title: Cats in Ancient Egypt: Gods, Mystery, and Worship 🐱
description: The ancient Egyptians worshipped cats as incarnations of the goddess Bastet. Discover why cats were considered sacred. Thousands of mummified cats were found in archaeological excavations. The story of how cats were revered in one of the most remarkable civilizations.

segments.voice: Cats have a long history in Egypt dating back over 5,000 years. The ancient Egyptians were probably the first civilization to widely domesticate cats for companionship, not just for hunting.
The goddess Bastet, also known as Bast, was the most important feline deity in the Egyptian pantheon. Originally represented as a fierce lioness, Bastet was eventually depicted as a domestic cat.
Bastet was the goddess of fertility, motherhood, home protection, and joy. The ancient Egyptians believed that owning a cat placed you under her divine protection.

In Bubastis, a major city in the Nile delta, there existed a temple dedicated to Bastet. This temple was one of the most visited religious sites in Egypt, attracting pilgrims from all over the empire.
Historians estimate that over 1 million pilgrims visited the temple of Bastet annually. It was a place of celebration, healing, and worship during festivals dedicated to the goddess.
Killing a cat in Ancient Egypt was a crime punishable by death. Laws protected cats with the same level of seriousness as they protected members of the royal family.
The Greek historian Herodotus, who visited Egypt around 450 BCE, documented that when a cat died, the family would shave their eyebrows in mourning. Cats were fully valued members of the family.
Thousands of cats were mummified and buried as religious offerings. Archaeologists have found entire cemeteries dedicated solely to cats in sites like Saqqara.
In an excavation in Saqqara in 2020, archaeologists discovered over 100 mummified cats, some still inside their original limestone urns. Some dated back over 2,500 years.
Cats were mummified with the same careful attention as humans. Organs were removed, the body was treated with natron to preserve it, and it was wrapped in fine linen.
The process of mummifying cats was so complex that archaeologists can date mummified cats by examining wrapping techniques and burial materials.
In the British Museum in London, there is a collection of thousands of bronze cat statuettes from Ancient Egypt. Each was a religious gift or an object of personal devotion.
Cats also had a practical purpose in Egypt. They protected grain stores from rodents, ensuring that the grain for harvest remained safe. This practical utility combined with religious devotion.
Pharaoh Pepi II, who ruled around 2278 BCE, is famous for having a pet cat. Ancient records show that his cat was so beloved that it was given the honors of a member of the royal family.
Egyptian papyri depict cats in everyday life: sitting on family tables, accompanying hunters, and being active players in domestic life.
When invading powers conquered Egypt, the worship of cats began to disappear. The Romans, although they appreciated cats, did not worship them in the same way.
The legacy of cats in Egypt is permanent. It influenced how the world saw cats for centuries. The connection between cats and the divine remains in culture to this day.
In conclusion, cats were not just pets in Ancient Egypt. They were living gods, honored family members, and symbols of the divine. A story that shows how long humans have loved cats.
